title: Trigonometric Functions of Any Angle
objectives: Define all six trigonometric functions (sin, cos, tan, csc, sec, cot)
Understand the reciprocal and quotient relationships between functions
Evaluate trigonometric functions using a point on the terminal side
Apply the Pythagorean identities to simplify expressions
Determine the domain and range of each trigonometric function
Identify where each function is undefined

devotional.title: Six Functions, One Truth
devotional.content:
The six trigonometric functions—sine, cosine, tangent, and their 
reciprocals—seem at first like six separate concepts. But as we've seen, 
they're deeply interconnected. Knowing one function and the quadrant 
determines all the others. They're bound together by the Pythagorean 
identities, the reciprocal relationships, and the quotient identities.

This mathematical unity mirrors a spiritual truth. Christianity reveals 
God as Trinity—Father, Son, and Holy Spirit—three persons, yet one 
God. What seems like three (or, in the case of trig, six) is actually 
a unified whole. The persons of the Trinity, like the trig functions, 
cannot be fully understood in isolation. Each relates to and reveals 
the others.

The Pythagorean identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ encodes the 
constraint that every point lies on the unit circle. No matter the 
angle, no matter the quadrant, this identity holds. It's a mathematical 
constant—a truth that doesn't change based on circumstance.

"Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever" (Hebrews 13:8). 
In a world of change, of angles large and small, of positive and negative 
directions, the character of God remains constant. The identities of 
trigonometry are true for all angles. The identity of Christ is true 
for all time.

As you memorize the signs of each function in each quadrant, as you learn 
to navigate from sine to cosecant, remember: mathematical consistency 
points to something deeper. The universe holds together because a 
consistent God holds it together.

epigraph.text:
*"The heavens declare the glory of God; the skies 
proclaim the work of his hands."*
epigraph.quote: Trigonometry is the soul of astronomy.

content.text:
The unit circle gives us sine and cosine, but mathematics has six 
trigonometric functions, not two. In this section, we define all six 
functions, explore their fundamental identities, and learn to evaluate 
them for any angle. These six functions—sine, cosine, tangent, 
cotangent, secant, and cosecant—form a complete toolkit for describing 
angles and rotations.

We've already defined sine and cosine using the unit circle: if the 
terminal side of angle $\theta$ meets the unit circle at $(x, y)$, then 
$\cos\theta = x$ and $\sin\theta = y$. The other four functions are 
built from these two.

content.title: The Six Trigonometric Functions
content.content:
For an angle $\theta$ with terminal point $(x, y)$ on the unit circle:
| **Primary Functions** | **Reciprocal Functions** |
| $\sin\theta = y$ | $\csc\theta = \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta} = \dfrac{1}{y}$ (provided $y \neq 0$) |
| $\cos\theta = x$ | $\sec\theta = \dfrac{1}{\cos\theta} = \dfrac{1}{x}$ (provided $x \neq 0$) |
| $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \dfrac{y}{x}$ ($x \neq 0$) | $\cot\theta = \dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = \dfrac{x}{y}$ ($y \neq 0$) |

content.caption:
Geometric interpretation of the six trigonometric functions. On the unit circle, 
$\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ are the $x$ and $y$ coordinates of point $P$. The tangent 
equals the length of the vertical segment from $(1,0)$ to where the terminal side 
(extended) meets the vertical line $x=1$. The secant is the distance from the origin 
to this intersection point.
content.alt:
Geometric interpretation of the six trigonometric functions. On the unit circle, 
$\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ are the $x$ and $y$ coordinates of point $P$. The tangent 
equals the length of the vertical segment from $(1,0)$ to where the terminal side 
(extended) meets the vertical line $x=1$. The secant is the distance from the origin 
to this intersection point.

items: Cosecant (csc) is the reciprocal of sine
Secant (sec) is the reciprocal of cosine
Cotangent (cot) is the reciprocal of tangent

content.content:
Common notation pitfall: $\sin^{-1}x$ does NOT mean $\frac{1}{\sin x}$. 
The notation $\sin^{-1}$ refers to the inverse sine function (arcsine), 
which we'll study later. The reciprocal of $\sin x$ is $\csc x$.

content.text:
Since tangent and secant involve division by $\cos\theta = x$, they are 
undefined when $\cos\theta = 0$ (i.e., when the terminal side lies on 
the $y$-axis).

Since cotangent and cosecant involve division by $\sin\theta = y$, they 
are undefined when $\sin\theta = 0$ (i.e., when the terminal side lies 
on the $x$-axis).

| **Function** | **Undefined when** |
| $\tan\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2} + n\pi$ (odd multiples of $\frac{\pi}{2}$) |
| $\cot\theta$ | $\theta = n\pi$ (multiples of $\pi$) |
| $\sec\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2} + n\pi$ |
| $\csc\theta$ | $\theta = n\pi$ |

content.title: Evaluating all six functions at a quadrantal angle
content.problem: Evaluate all six trigonometric functions at $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$.
content.solution:
At $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$, the terminal point on the unit circle is $(0, 1)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
 \sin\frac{\pi}{2} &= y = 1 & \cos\frac{\pi}{2} &= x = 0 \\[4pt]
 \tan\frac{\pi}{2} &= \frac{y}{x} = \frac{1}{0} = \text{undefined} & 
 \cot\frac{\pi}{2} &= \frac{x}{y} = \frac{0}{1} = 0 \\[4pt]
 \csc\frac{\pi}{2} &= \frac{1}{y} = \frac{1}{1} = 1 & 
 \sec\frac{\pi}{2} &= \frac{1}{x} = \frac{1}{0} = \text{undefined}
\end{aligned}$$

content.text: Evaluating All Six Functions for Special Angles

Let's build a complete table of exact values for the special angles in 
the first quadrant.

content.title: All six functions for $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$ (30°)
content.problem: Find all six trigonometric values for $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{6}$.
content.solution:
From the unit circle, the point at $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$ is $\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \dfrac{1}{2}\right)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
 \sin\frac{\pi}{6} &= \frac{1}{2} & 
 \csc\frac{\pi}{6} &= \frac{1}{1/2} = 2 \\[6pt]
 \cos\frac{\pi}{6} &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} & 
 \sec\frac{\pi}{6} &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}/2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3} \\[6pt]
 \tan\frac{\pi}{6} &= \frac{1/2}{\sqrt{3}/2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{3} & 
 \cot\frac{\pi}{6} &= \frac{\sqrt{3}/2}{1/2} = \sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: All six functions for $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$ (45°)
content.problem: Find all six trigonometric values for $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$.
content.solution:
The point at $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ is $\left(\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
 \sin\frac{\pi}{4} &= \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} & 
 \csc\frac{\pi}{4} &= \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \\[6pt]
 \cos\frac{\pi}{4} &= \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} & 
 \sec\frac{\pi}{4} &= \frac{2}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \\[6pt]
 \tan\frac{\pi}{4} &= \frac{\sqrt{2}/2}{\sqrt{2}/2} = 1 & 
 \cot\frac{\pi}{4} &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

Notice that at 45°, sine equals cosine, so tangent and cotangent are both 1, 
and secant equals cosecant.

content.title: All six functions for $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$ (60°)
content.problem: Find all six trigonometric values for $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{3}$.

content.text: Signs of Trigonometric Functions by Quadrant

We know how sine and cosine behave in each quadrant. What about the 
other four functions?

items:
Tangent is positive when sine and cosine have the same sign 
 (both positive in QI, both negative in QIII)
Tangent is negative when they have opposite signs (QII and QIV)

content.text: The reciprocal functions have the same signs as their "parent" functions.

content.title: ASTC: A Memory Aid for Signs
content.content:
A common mnemonic for remembering which functions are positive in each 
quadrant is **ASTC**:
| **A**ll | — All functions positive in Quadrant I |
| **S**tudents | — Sine (and cosecant) positive in Quadrant II |
| **T**ake | — Tangent (and cotangent) positive in Quadrant III |
| **C**alculus | — Cosine (and secant) positive in Quadrant IV |

Some students prefer "All Students Take Classes" or "All Silly Tom Cats." 
Choose whatever helps you remember!

content.caption:
The ASTC rule: **A**ll functions positive in QI, **S**ine in QII, 
**T**angent in QIII, **C**osine in QIV. Reciprocal functions share signs 
with their parent functions.
content.alt:
The ASTC rule: **A**ll functions positive in QI, **S**ine in QII, 
**T**angent in QIII, **C**osine in QIV. Reciprocal functions share signs 
with their parent functions.

content.text:
Recall from Section that the **reference angle** 
$\theta'$ for any angle $\theta$ is the acute angle formed between the 
terminal side of $\theta$ and the $x$-axis. Reference angles let us reduce 
any trigonometric evaluation to a first-quadrant problem.

content.caption:
Reference angles in all four quadrants. The reference angle $\theta'$ (shown in coral) 
is always the acute angle between the terminal side and the $x$-axis. In each quadrant, 
a different formula converts $\theta$ to its reference angle.
content.alt:
Reference angles in all four quadrants. The reference angle $\theta'$ (shown in coral) 
is always the acute angle between the terminal side and the $x$-axis. In each quadrant, 
a different formula converts $\theta$ to its reference angle.

content.title: All six functions in Quadrant III
content.problem: Find all six trigonometric values for $\theta = \dfrac{5\pi}{4}$.
content.solution:
**Step 1:** Identify quadrant and reference angle.

$\dfrac{5\pi}{4} = 225°$ is in Quadrant III.

Reference angle: $\dfrac{5\pi}{4} - \pi = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$ (45°).

**Step 2:** Find sine and cosine.

In QIII, both are negative:
$$\begin{aligned}
 \sin\frac{5\pi}{4} &= -\sin\frac{\pi}{4} = -\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \\
 \cos\frac{5\pi}{4} &= -\cos\frac{\pi}{4} = -\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the remaining functions.
$$\begin{aligned}
 \tan\frac{5\pi}{4} &= \frac{-\sqrt{2}/2}{-\sqrt{2}/2} = 1 
 & \cot\frac{5\pi}{4} &= 1 \\[4pt]
 \csc\frac{5\pi}{4} &= \frac{1}{-\sqrt{2}/2} = -\sqrt{2} 
 & \sec\frac{5\pi}{4} &= \frac{1}{-\sqrt{2}/2} = -\sqrt{2}
\end{aligned}$$

In QIII, tangent and cotangent are positive; cosecant and secant are negative.

content.title: All six functions in Quadrant II
content.problem: Find all six trigonometric values for $\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$.

content.text:
The six trigonometric functions are not independent—they're connected 
by a web of identities. These relationships are essential for simplifying 
expressions and solving equations.

content.title: Deriving the second Pythagorean identity
content.problem: Derive $1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta$ from $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$.
content.solution:
Start with the basic Pythagorean identity:
$$
 \sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1
$$

Divide both sides by $\cos^2\theta$ (assuming $\cos\theta \neq 0$):
$$
 \frac{\sin^2\theta}{\cos^2\theta} + \frac{\cos^2\theta}{\cos^2\theta} = \frac{1}{\cos^2\theta}
$$

Using the definitions $\tan\theta = \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$ and 
$\sec\theta = \frac{1}{\cos\theta}$:
$$
 \tan^2\theta + 1 = \sec^2\theta
$$

Or equivalently: $1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta$ ✓

content.text: Finding All Six Functions from One Value

A powerful application of the fundamental identities: given one 
trigonometric value and the quadrant, we can find all five other values.

content.title: Finding all values from sine
content.problem:
If $\sin\theta = \dfrac{3}{5}$ and $\theta$ is in Quadrant I, find the 
other five trigonometric values.
content.solution:
**Step 1: Find cosine using the Pythagorean identity.**
$$
 \sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1
$$
$$
 \left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^2 + \cos^2\theta = 1
$$
$$
 \frac{9}{25} + \cos^2\theta = 1 \implies \cos^2\theta = \frac{16}{25}
$$

Since $\theta$ is in QI, cosine is positive: $\cos\theta = \dfrac{4}{5}$.

**Step 2: Find the remaining functions.**
$$\begin{aligned}
 \tan\theta &= \frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = \frac{3/5}{4/5} = \frac{3}{4} \\[6pt]
 \cot\theta &= \frac{1}{\tan\theta} = \frac{4}{3} \\[6pt]
 \csc\theta &= \frac{1}{\sin\theta} = \frac{5}{3} \\[6pt]
 \sec\theta &= \frac{1}{\cos\theta} = \frac{5}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Finding all values from cosine in Quadrant III
content.problem:
If $\cos\theta = -\dfrac{5}{13}$ and $\theta$ is in Quadrant III, find 
all six trigonometric values.
content.solution:
**Step 1: Find sine.**
$$
 \sin^2\theta = 1 - \cos^2\theta = 1 - \frac{25}{169} = \frac{144}{169}
$$

In QIII, sine is negative: $\sin\theta = -\dfrac{12}{13}$.

**Step 2: Find the other functions.**
$$\begin{aligned}
 \tan\theta &= \frac{-12/13}{-5/13} = \frac{12}{5} & 
 \cot\theta &= \frac{5}{12} \\[6pt]
 \csc\theta &= -\frac{13}{12} & 
 \sec\theta &= -\frac{13}{5}
\end{aligned}$$

Note that tangent and cotangent are positive in QIII (as expected).

content.title: Finding all values from tangent
content.problem:
If $\tan\theta = -\dfrac{7}{24}$ and $\cos\theta > 0$, find all six 
trigonometric values.
content.solution:
**Step 1: Determine the quadrant.**

Tangent is negative and cosine is positive. This means $\theta$ is in QIV.

**Step 2: Use the Pythagorean identity for tangent.**
$$
 1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta
$$
$$
 1 + \frac{49}{576} = \sec^2\theta = \frac{576 + 49}{576} = \frac{625}{576}
$$

In QIV, secant is positive: $\sec\theta = \dfrac{25}{24}$.

Therefore: $\cos\theta = \dfrac{24}{25}$.

**Step 3: Find sine from cosine.**
$$
 \sin^2\theta = 1 - \cos^2\theta = 1 - \frac{576}{625} = \frac{49}{625}
$$

In QIV, sine is negative: $\sin\theta = -\dfrac{7}{25}$.

**Step 4: Find the remaining functions.**
$$\begin{aligned}
 \csc\theta &= -\frac{25}{7} & \cot\theta &= -\frac{24}{7}
\end{aligned}$$

**Verification:** $\tan\theta = \frac{-7/25}{24/25} = -\frac{7}{24}$ ✓

content.text:
Some functions are symmetric about the $y$-axis (even functions), while 
others are symmetric about the origin (odd functions). These properties 
help us evaluate trig functions at negative angles.

content.title: Even-Odd Properties of Trig Functions
content.content:
**Even function** (symmetric about the $y$-axis):
$$
 \cos(-\theta) = \cos\theta \qquad \sec(-\theta) = \sec\theta
$$

**Odd functions** (symmetric about the origin):
$$\begin{aligned}
 \sin(-\theta) &= -\sin\theta & \csc(-\theta) &= -\csc\theta \\
 \tan(-\theta) &= -\tan\theta & \cot(-\theta) &= -\cot\theta
\end{aligned}$$

content.caption:
Even-odd symmetry visualized. Reflecting angle $\theta$ across the $x$-axis 
gives $-\theta$. Points $P$ and $Q$ have the *same* $x$-coordinate (so $\cos(-\theta) = \cos\theta$) 
but *opposite* $y$-coordinates (so $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin\theta$).
content.alt:
Even-odd symmetry visualized. Reflecting angle $\theta$ across the $x$-axis 
gives $-\theta$. Points $P$ and $Q$ have the *same* $x$-coordinate (so $\cos(-\theta) = \cos\theta$) 
but *opposite* $y$-coordinates (so $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin\theta$).

content.title: Using even-odd properties
content.problem:
Use even-odd properties and the unit circle to evaluate:

1. $\sin(-\dfrac{\pi}{6})$

2. $\cos(-\dfrac{\pi}{4})$

3. $\tan(-\dfrac{\pi}{3})$
content.solution:
1. Sine is odd: $\sin(-\dfrac{\pi}{6}) = -\sin\dfrac{\pi}{6} = -\dfrac{1}{2}$

2. Cosine is even: $\cos(-\dfrac{\pi}{4}) = \cos\dfrac{\pi}{4} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$

3. Tangent is odd: $\tan(-\dfrac{\pi}{3}) = -\tan\dfrac{\pi}{3} = -\sqrt{3}$

content.text: Extending to Points Not on the Unit Circle

Sometimes we're given a point $(x, y)$ on the terminal side of an angle 
that isn't on the unit circle. We can still find all trigonometric values.

content.title: Trig Functions from Any Point
content.content:
If point $(x, y)$ lies on the terminal side of angle $\theta$ in standard 
position, and $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ is the distance from $(x, y)$ to 
the origin, then:
$$
 \sin\theta = \frac{y}{r} \qquad \cos\theta = \frac{x}{r} \qquad \tan\theta = \frac{y}{x}
$$
$$
 \csc\theta = \frac{r}{y} \qquad \sec\theta = \frac{r}{x} \qquad \cot\theta = \frac{x}{y}
$$

content.caption:
Trigonometric functions from any point. Given point $(x, y)$ on the 
terminal side of $\theta$, we compute $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ and use the 
definitions $\sin\theta = y/r$, $\cos\theta = x/r$, $\tan\theta = y/x$.
content.alt:
Trigonometric functions from any point. Given point $(x, y)$ on the 
terminal side of $\theta$, we compute $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ and use the 
definitions $\sin\theta = y/r$, $\cos\theta = x/r$, $\tan\theta = y/x$.

content.title: Trig values from a point not on the unit circle
content.problem:
The terminal side of angle $\theta$ passes through $(3, -4)$. Find all 
six trigonometric values.
content.solution:
First, find $r$:
$$
 r = \sqrt{3^2 + (-4)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5
$$

Now compute all six values:
$$\begin{aligned}
 \sin\theta &= \frac{y}{r} = \frac{-4}{5} = -\frac{4}{5} & 
 \csc\theta &= \frac{r}{y} = -\frac{5}{4} \\[4pt]
 \cos\theta &= \frac{x}{r} = \frac{3}{5} & 
 \sec\theta &= \frac{r}{x} = \frac{5}{3} \\[4pt]
 \tan\theta &= \frac{y}{x} = \frac{-4}{3} = -\frac{4}{3} & 
 \cot\theta &= \frac{x}{y} = -\frac{3}{4}
\end{aligned}$$

Note: $(3, -4)$ is in QIV, where sine and tangent are negative, cosine 
is positive. Our answers match!

content.title: Working with a point in Quadrant II
content.problem:
The terminal side of angle $\theta$ passes through $(-12, 5)$. Find 
$\sin\theta$, $\cos\theta$, and $\tan\theta$.

content.title: Trigonometric Functions of Any Angle
content.content:
- The six trigonometric functions are: $\sin$, $\cos$, $\tan$, 
 $\csc$, $\sec$, $\cot$.

- **Reciprocal identities:** $\csc\theta = 1/\sin\theta$, 
 $\sec\theta = 1/\cos\theta$, $\cot\theta = 1/\tan\theta$.

- **Quotient identities:** $\tan\theta = \sin\theta/\cos\theta$, 
 $\cot\theta = \cos\theta/\sin\theta$.

- **Pythagorean identities:**
 
- - $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$

- $1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta$

- $1 + \cot^2\theta = \csc^2\theta$

- **Even/Odd:** Cosine and secant are even; the other four 
 are odd.

- Given one trig value and the quadrant, you can find all others.

content.problem:
Fill in the blank with the appropriate term.

1. The of sine is cosecant.

2. If $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$, this is called a identity.

3. The identity $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$ is called the identity.

4. Cosine is an function because $\cos(-\theta) = \cos\theta$.

5. Sine is an function because $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin\theta$.

6. The functions $\tan$ and $\sec$ are undefined when $\cos\theta = $ .

Match each function with its reciprocal.

1. $\sin\theta$ A. $\cot\theta$

2. $\cos\theta$ B. $\csc\theta$

3. $\tan\theta$ C. $\sec\theta$

Evaluate all six trigonometric functions at each angle.

1. $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ (b) $\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$ 
 (c) $\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$ (d) $\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$

Evaluate all six trigonometric functions at each angle.

1. $\dfrac{7\pi}{6}$ (b) $\dfrac{4\pi}{3}$ 
 (c) $\dfrac{5\pi}{3}$ (d) $\dfrac{11\pi}{6}$

Evaluate all six trigonometric functions at each angle.

1. $0$ (b) $\pi$ (c) $-\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ (d) $2\pi$

If $\cos\theta = -\dfrac{5}{13}$ and $\theta$ is in Quadrant III, find 
$\sin\theta$ and $\tan\theta$.

If $\sin\theta = \dfrac{8}{17}$ and $\theta$ is in Quadrant II, find all 
six trigonometric values.
If $\tan\theta = 2$ and $\sin\theta < 0$, find $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$.
If $\sec\theta = -3$ and $\tan\theta > 0$, find $\sin\theta$.

If $\cot\theta = -\dfrac{3}{4}$ and $\cos\theta > 0$, find all six 
trigonometric values.

Simplify each expression.

1. $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta$

2. $\sec^2\theta - \tan^2\theta$

3. $\csc^2\theta - \cot^2\theta$

4. $\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\csc\theta} + \dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sec\theta}$

Verify each identity by transforming one side into the other.

1. $\tan\theta \cos\theta = \sin\theta$

2. $\cot\theta \sin\theta = \cos\theta$

3. $\sin\theta \sec\theta = \tan\theta$

4. $\cos\theta \csc\theta = \cot\theta$

Write each expression in terms of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$, then simplify.

1. $\tan\theta \cot\theta$

2. $\sec\theta \cos\theta$

3. $\dfrac{\tan\theta}{\sec\theta}$

4. $\dfrac{1 - \sin^2\theta}{\cos\theta}$

Use even-odd properties to find each value.

1. $\sin(-\dfrac{\pi}{6})$ (b) $\cos(-\dfrac{\pi}{4})$ 
 (c) $\tan(-\dfrac{\pi}{3})$ (d) $\sec(-\dfrac{\pi}{3})$

If $\sin\theta = 0.6$ and $\cos\theta = 0.8$, find:

1. $\sin(-\theta)$ (b) $\cos(-\theta)$ (c) $\tan(-\theta)$

The terminal side of $\theta$ passes through the given point. Find all 
six trigonometric values.

1. $(5, 12)$ (b) $(-8, 15)$ (c) $(-7, -24)$ (d) $(4, -3)$

The terminal side of $\theta$ passes through $(-2, 5)$. Find $\sin\theta$, 
$\cos\theta$, and $\tan\theta$. Express answers in simplified radical form.

Explain why $\tan\theta$ is undefined when $\theta = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$ but 
$\cot\theta$ equals 0 at the same angle.

If $\sin\theta = k$, express each in terms of $k$:

1. $\sin(-\theta)$

2. $\csc\theta$

3. $\sin(\theta + 2\pi)$
Prove: $\dfrac{1 - \sin^2\theta}{\cos\theta} = \cos\theta$
Prove: $\sec^2\theta + \csc^2\theta = \sec^2\theta \csc^2\theta$
If $\sin\theta + \cos\theta = 1$, find all possible values of $\sin\theta\cos\theta$.

Find the exact values of the six trigonometric functions for an angle 
$\theta$ whose terminal side lies on the line $y = 2x$ in Quadrant III.

Evaluate all six trigonometric functions at each quadrantal angle.

1. $0$

2. $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$

3. $\pi$

4. $\dfrac{3\pi}{2}$

Evaluate all six trigonometric functions at each angle.

1. $\dfrac{\pi}{6}$

2. $\dfrac{\pi}{3}$

3. $\dfrac{5\pi}{4}$

4. $\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$

Evaluate all six trigonometric functions at each angle.

1. $\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$

2. $\dfrac{5\pi}{3}$

3. $\dfrac{7\pi}{6}$

4. $\dfrac{11\pi}{6}$

1. $-\dfrac{\pi}{6}$

2. $-\dfrac{\pi}{4}$

3. $-\dfrac{\pi}{3}$

4. $-\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$

Evaluate each expression without a calculator.

1. $\tan\dfrac{\pi}{4} + \cot\dfrac{\pi}{4}$

2. $\sec\dfrac{\pi}{3} - \csc\dfrac{\pi}{6}$

3. $\sin^2\dfrac{\pi}{6} + \cos^2\dfrac{\pi}{6}$

4. $\tan^2\dfrac{\pi}{4} + 1$

Evaluate each expression without a calculator.

1. $\csc\dfrac{\pi}{2} + \sec 0$

2. $\tan\dfrac{\pi}{3} \cdot \cot\dfrac{\pi}{3}$

3. $\sin\dfrac{\pi}{3} \cdot \csc\dfrac{\pi}{3}$

4. $\cos\dfrac{5\pi}{6} + \sin\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$

content.text: Signs of Trig Functions by Quadrant

content.problem:
State the sign (positive or negative) of each function value.

1. $\sin 150°$

2. $\cos 200°$

3. $\tan 300°$

4. $\csc 250°$

5. $\sec 100°$

6. $\cot 330°$

State the sign (positive or negative) of each function value.

1. $\sin\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$

2. $\cos\dfrac{5\pi}{4}$

3. $\tan\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$

4. $\sec\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$

5. $\csc\dfrac{4\pi}{3}$

6. $\cot\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$

Name the quadrant(s) in which $\theta$ could lie.

1. $\sin\theta > 0$ and $\cos\theta > 0$

2. $\sin\theta < 0$ and $\tan\theta > 0$

3. $\cos\theta < 0$ and $\csc\theta > 0$

4. $\sec\theta < 0$ and $\cot\theta < 0$

5. $\tan\theta > 0$

6. $\csc\theta < 0$

Name the quadrant(s) in which $\theta$ could lie.

1. $\sin\theta > 0$ and $\tan\theta < 0$

2. $\cos\theta > 0$ and $\cot\theta < 0$

3. $\sec\theta > 0$ and $\csc\theta < 0$

4. $\tan\theta < 0$ and $\csc\theta < 0$

Find all six trigonometric values given the information.

1. $\sin\theta = \dfrac{5}{13}$, $\theta$ in Quadrant I

2. $\cos\theta = \dfrac{3}{5}$, $\theta$ in Quadrant IV

3. $\tan\theta = -\dfrac{4}{3}$, $\theta$ in Quadrant II

4. $\csc\theta = -\dfrac{5}{3}$, $\theta$ in Quadrant III

Find all six trigonometric values given the information.

1. $\sin\theta = -\dfrac{7}{25}$, $\cos\theta > 0$

2. $\cos\theta = -\dfrac{8}{17}$, $\sin\theta > 0$

3. $\tan\theta = \dfrac{5}{12}$, $\sin\theta < 0$

4. $\sec\theta = \dfrac{13}{5}$, $\tan\theta < 0$

Find $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ given the information.

1. $\tan\theta = 3$, $\theta$ in Quadrant III

2. $\cot\theta = -2$, $\theta$ in Quadrant IV

3. $\sec\theta = -\sqrt{5}$, $\tan\theta > 0$

4. $\csc\theta = 4$, $\cos\theta < 0$

Find $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ given the information.

1. $\tan\theta = -\dfrac{1}{2}$, $\sec\theta > 0$

2. $\cot\theta = \dfrac{3}{4}$, $\csc\theta < 0$

3. $\sec\theta = 2$, $\sin\theta < 0$

4. $\csc\theta = -\dfrac{5}{4}$, $\cot\theta > 0$

The terminal side of $\theta$ passes through the given point. Find all six trigonometric values.

1. $(3, 4)$

2. $(8, 15)$

3. $(5, -12)$

4. $(-20, 21)$

The terminal side of $\theta$ passes through the given point. Find all six trigonometric values.

1. $(-9, 40)$

2. $(-7, -24)$

3. $(1, -1)$

4. $(-3, 3)$

The terminal side of $\theta$ passes through the given point. Find $\sin\theta$, $\cos\theta$, and $\tan\theta$. Leave answers in simplified radical form.

1. $(1, 2)$

2. $(-2, 1)$

3. $(3, -1)$

4. $(-1, -3)$

The terminal side of $\theta$ passes through the given point. Find all six trigonometric values. Rationalize denominators.

1. $(2, 3)$

2. $(-4, 1)$

3. $(5, -2)$

4. $(-3, -4)$

Simplify each expression to a single trigonometric function or constant.

1. $\dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta} \cdot \cot\theta$

2. $\sec\theta \cdot \cos\theta$

3. $\tan\theta \cdot \csc\theta \cdot \cos\theta$

4. $\dfrac{\csc\theta}{\sec\theta}$

Simplify each expression to a single trigonometric function or constant.

1. $\dfrac{\tan\theta}{\sin\theta}$

2. $\dfrac{\cot\theta}{\cos\theta}$

3. $\sin\theta \cdot \sec\theta$

4. $\cos\theta \cdot \csc\theta$

Write each expression in terms of sine and cosine, then simplify.

1. $\dfrac{\tan\theta + \cot\theta}{\csc\theta}$

2. $\dfrac{\sec\theta - \cos\theta}{\tan\theta}$

3. $\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta} - \dfrac{1}{\csc\theta}$

4. $\sec^2\theta - \tan^2\theta$

Simplify using the Pythagorean identities.

1. $\dfrac{1 - \cos^2\theta}{\sin\theta}$

2. $\dfrac{\sec^2\theta - 1}{\tan\theta}$

3. $\dfrac{\csc^2\theta - 1}{\cot\theta}$

4. $\sin^2\theta(1 + \cot^2\theta)$

Use even-odd properties to evaluate.

1. $\cos(-30°)$

2. $\sin(-45°)$

3. $\tan(-60°)$

4. $\sec(-45°)$

5. $\csc(-30°)$

6. $\cot(-60°)$

Use even-odd properties to evaluate.

1. $\sin(-\dfrac{\pi}{4})$

2. $\cos(-\dfrac{\pi}{3})$

3. $\tan(-\dfrac{\pi}{6})$

4. $\sec(-\dfrac{\pi}{4})$

5. $\csc(-\dfrac{\pi}{3})$

6. $\cot(-\dfrac{\pi}{4})$

If $\sin\theta = 0.4$ and $\cos\theta = 0.9$, find each value.

1. $\sin(-\theta)$

2. $\cos(-\theta)$

3. $\tan(-\theta)$

4. $-\sin\theta$

5. $-\cos\theta$

6. $\tan(\theta)$

If $\tan\theta = -3$ and $\sec\theta = \sqrt{10}$, find each value.

1. $\tan(-\theta)$

2. $\sec(-\theta)$

3. $\cot(-\theta)$

4. $\cos(-\theta)$

From a point 200 feet from the base of a building, the angle of elevation to the top is $\theta$. If $\tan\theta = \dfrac{3}{4}$, find:

1. The height of the building

2. The distance from the observation point to the top of the building

3. The values of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$

A 25-foot ladder leans against a wall. If the ladder makes an angle $\theta$ with the ground where $\cos\theta = \dfrac{7}{25}$, find:

1. How far the base of the ladder is from the wall

2. How high up the wall the ladder reaches

3. The values of all six trig functions for $\theta$

A ship sails from port on a bearing where $\sin\theta = \dfrac{5}{13}$ and $\theta$ is in Quadrant I. After traveling 260 miles:

1. How far east has the ship traveled?

2. How far north has the ship traveled?

A surveyor measures the angle of elevation from ground level to the top of a cliff. The terminal side of the angle passes through the point $(120, 50)$ where distances are in feet.

1. Find $\tan\theta$ for the angle.

2. Find the distance from the surveyor to the top of the cliff.

3. Find $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$.

An object rests on an inclined plane. The component of gravitational force parallel to the plane is $mg\sin\theta$ and perpendicular is $mg\cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle of inclination. If $\tan\theta = \dfrac{3}{5}$:

1. Find $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$

2. If $mg = 100$ N, find each component force

A ball is thrown at an angle $\theta$ to the ground. The horizontal velocity is $v\cos\theta$ and vertical velocity is $v\sin\theta$. If $\sin\theta = 0.6$ and the initial speed is 50 m/s:

1. Find $\cos\theta$

2. Find the horizontal and vertical components of velocity

Use a calculator to evaluate each expression. Round to four decimal places.

1. $\tan 73°$

2. $\csc 28°$

3. $\sec 152°$

4. $\cot 245°$

5. $\sin 1.2$ (radians)

6. $\cos 2.8$ (radians)

Use a calculator to verify each identity for $\theta = 37°$.

1. $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$

2. $1 + \tan^2\theta = \sec^2\theta$

3. $1 + \cot^2\theta = \csc^2\theta$

4. $\tan\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}$

Use a graphing utility to create tables of values for $\sin\theta$, $\cos\theta$, and $\tan\theta$ from $0°$ to $360°$ in $30°$ increments. Identify:

1. Where each function is zero

2. Where each function is undefined

3. The maximum and minimum values of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$
Graph $y = \sin x$, $y = \csc x$, and their sum $y = \sin x + \csc x$ on the same axes for $0 < x < 2\pi$. Describe the relationship between the graphs.

True or False? Justify your answer.

1. For all angles $\theta$, $\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$.

2. If $\sin\theta > 0$, then $\theta$ must be in Quadrant I.

3. $\tan 90°$ is undefined because division by zero is undefined.

4. If $\tan\theta = 1$, then $\theta = 45°$.

5. $\sec\theta$ is always greater than or equal to 1.

6. $\csc\theta$ can equal $\dfrac{1}{2}$.

True or False? Justify your answer.

1. $\sin(-\theta) = -\sin\theta$ for all $\theta$.

2. $\cos(-\theta) = -\cos\theta$ for all $\theta$.

3. If $\cos\theta = 0$, then $\sin\theta = \pm 1$.

4. The range of $\tan\theta$ is $[-1, 1]$.

5. $|\sec\theta| \geq 1$ for all $\theta$ where $\sec\theta$ is defined.

6. If $\sin\theta = \cos\theta$, then $\tan\theta = 1$.

Explain why:

1. $\csc\theta$ can never equal $\dfrac{1}{2}$

2. $|\sec\theta| \geq 1$ whenever $\sec\theta$ is defined

3. $\tan\theta$ and $\cot\theta$ can take any real value

4. If $\sin\theta = 1$, then $\cos\theta$ must equal $0$

Without calculating, determine which is larger:

1. $\sin 40°$ or $\sin 50°$

2. $\cos 40°$ or $\cos 50°$

3. $\tan 40°$ or $\tan 50°$

4. $\sec 40°$ or $\sec 50°$
Explain your reasoning.

Describe and correct the error.

Finding $\cos\theta$ when $\sin\theta = \dfrac{3}{5}$ and $\theta$ is in Quadrant II:
> "$\sin^2\theta + \cos^2\theta = 1$

$\dfrac{9}{25} + \cos^2\theta = 1$

$\cos^2\theta = \dfrac{16}{25}$

$\cos\theta = \dfrac{4}{5}$" ✗

Describe and correct the error.

Finding all six trig values when the terminal side passes through $(3, -4)$:
> "$r = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5$

$\sin\theta = \dfrac{3}{5}$, $\cos\theta = \dfrac{-4}{5}$, $\tan\theta = \dfrac{3}{-4} = -\dfrac{3}{4}$" ✗

Describe and correct the error.

Evaluating $\csc\dfrac{\pi}{3}$:
> "$\csc\dfrac{\pi}{3} = \dfrac{1}{\cos\frac{\pi}{3}} = \dfrac{1}{1/2} = 2$" ✗

If $\sin\theta + \cos\theta = \dfrac{7}{5}$, find:

1. $\sin\theta \cos\theta$

2. $\sin^3\theta + \cos^3\theta$
Find all angles $\theta$ with $0 \leq \theta < 2\pi$ such that $\sin\theta = \cos\theta$.
The terminal side of angle $\theta$ lies on the line $3x + 4y = 0$ with $x \geq 0$. Find all six trigonometric values of $\theta$.
If $\tan\theta + \sec\theta = 3$, find $\cos\theta$.

Prove that for any angle $\theta$ where all functions are defined:
$$
(\sin\theta + \cos\theta)^2 + (\sin\theta - \cos\theta)^2 = 2
$$

Find the exact value without a calculator (use reference angles).

1. $\sin\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$

2. $\cos\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$

3. $\tan\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$

4. $\sin\dfrac{5\pi}{4}$

5. $\cos\dfrac{4\pi}{3}$

6. $\tan\dfrac{7\pi}{6}$

7. $\sin\dfrac{5\pi}{3}$

8. $\cos\dfrac{11\pi}{6}$

9. $\tan\dfrac{7\pi}{4}$

Find the exact value of all six trig functions.

1. $\csc\dfrac{\pi}{6}$

2. $\sec\dfrac{\pi}{4}$

3. $\cot\dfrac{\pi}{3}$

4. $\csc\dfrac{2\pi}{3}$

5. $\sec\dfrac{5\pi}{6}$

6. $\cot\dfrac{5\pi}{4}$

Use even-odd properties to find the exact value.

1. $\sin(-\dfrac{\pi}{6})$

2. $\cos(-\dfrac{\pi}{4})$

3. $\tan(-\dfrac{\pi}{3})$

4. $\sin(-\dfrac{5\pi}{6})$

5. $\cos(-\dfrac{2\pi}{3})$

6. $\tan(-\dfrac{3\pi}{4})$

Find the exact values of the other five trig functions.

1. $\sin\theta = \dfrac{3}{5}$, $\theta$ in QI

2. $\cos\theta = -\dfrac{4}{5}$, $\theta$ in QII

3. $\tan\theta = \dfrac{5}{12}$, $\theta$ in QIII

4. $\sin\theta = -\dfrac{8}{17}$, $\theta$ in QIV

Find the exact values.

1. $\tan\theta = -\dfrac{3}{4}$, $\cos\theta > 0$; find $\sin\theta$

2. $\sec\theta = -\dfrac{5}{3}$, $\tan\theta > 0$; find $\sin\theta$

3. $\cot\theta = \dfrac{7}{24}$, $\sin\theta < 0$; find $\cos\theta$

4. $\csc\theta = -\dfrac{13}{5}$, $\cos\theta > 0$; find $\tan\theta$

Find the exact value (degree measure).

1. $\sin 240°$

2. $\cos 315°$

3. $\tan 150°$

4. $\csc 210°$

5. $\sec 300°$

6. $\cot 135°$

Find the exact value (radian measure).

1. $\sin \dfrac{11\pi}{6}$

2. $\cos \dfrac{7\pi}{4}$

3. $\tan \dfrac{4\pi}{3}$

4. $\csc \dfrac{5\pi}{6}$

5. $\sec \dfrac{5\pi}{3}$

6. $\cot \dfrac{7\pi}{6}$
If $\cos\theta = \dfrac{5}{13}$ and $\theta$ is in QIV, find all six trig functions.
If $\tan\theta = -2$ and $\sin\theta > 0$, find $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$.
If $\sec\theta = -3$ and $\tan\theta > 0$, find $\sin\theta$.
Find $\sin\dfrac{7\pi}{6}$.
Find $\cos(-60°)$.
Find $\tan\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$.
If $\sin\theta = \dfrac{2}{3}$ and $\theta$ is in QII, find $\cos\theta$.
Find $\cot 330°$.
Find $\sec\dfrac{4\pi}{3}$.
Find $\csc(-\dfrac{\pi}{3})$.
Find all $\theta$ in $[0, 2\pi)$ where $\sin\theta = \dfrac{1}{2}$.
Find all $\theta$ in $[0, 2\pi)$ where $\cos\theta = -\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.
Find all $\theta$ in $[0, 2\pi)$ where $\tan\theta = -1$.

marginNotes.text:
Do you know the laws of the heavens? Can you 
set up God's dominion over the earth?
